CURRENT AFFAIRS,

What trouble many of our readers, who have orders
to place, have to go to at times in search of a Dark
Brother even at this stage of the Industrial question.
A priest writes us from Britain :—‘‘ Dear Mr. Moran,—
Your delightful friends, the ‘Dark Brothers,’—it is
like a dream to me that some months ago you showed
{tem up advantageously in the case of some firm that
tizrned o=t scmee fine school desks, ete. I could put a good
crder in their way if I knew where to find them. If
any one in your office remembers, or could put his hand
cu their address and ask them to communicate with me,
it would be all in the good day’s work.”’

We have made enquiries and trust that they will re-
sult in getting this order sent to Ireland. - Why don’t
school furniture makers' and all other classes of manu-
facturers keep their names and addresses before our
readers? - We are glad to go to trouble to put a man
with an order into communication with some Irish firm
that could execute it. But though we get very many
inquiries, how many readers of the LEaDER who have

rders to give and not finding even a name and address

a manufacturer in the particular line, go to the

trouble of writing specially to us, and waiting until we
set out, as it were, with a dog and start a Dark
Brother. If Irish manufacturers were wise they would
all keep their names and addresses before the readers of
the LeapEr—the particular public who care about and
are interested in Irish industry, and who have con-
tracts to give and money to spend. Our readers spend
millions of pounds sterling every year, and the manu-
facturers ought to look after their share of it.

From Yorkshire, England, a correspondent writes :—
‘“ Dear Mr. Editor,—Pardon the liberty I take in writ-
ing to you, also for imposing upon your valuable time.
But I think you are the man to inform me. I have
been reading your articles on Irish industry in your good
little paper for some time, and I think that the Irish on
this side of the Channel could help Ireland a little by
making her manufactures better known. As for Irish
manufactured stuff, wools, etc., I will leave for some
better one than myself. I would like to get the names
and addresses of some dairymen over there, as I am
thinking of starting a dairy about here, and I would like
to do a little for my native land-if I could.”

The Tagoat (Co. Wexford) Cottage Gardening Com-
petition is an institution that other districts throughout
the country might establish. The competition is con-
fined to labourers residing in the parish of Tagoat. The
marks are awarded for the quantities and varieties of
vegetables, etc., as follows:—4 perches of potatoes, 10
marks; 4 perches of cabbage, 10 marks; 1 perch of
onions, 10 marks; 2 perches (drill) parsnips, 10 marks;
2 perches (drill) carrots, 10 marks; 2 perches (drill) pea,
10 marks; 2 perches (drill) broad beans, 10 marks; 1
perch any other vegetable at choice, 10 marks; fruit—
4 apples, 12 bush fruits and 30 strawberries, 10 marks;
fences, gates, flowers, general neatness, etc., 30 marks.
All the garden must be under cultivation. There
were' eleven entries this year, and five prizes were
awarded. Rev. Canon Doyle, P.P., to whose energy
the scheme is largely, if not altogether due, said in the
course of his remarks when announcing the results of the
competitions, that ‘‘ he was very pleased with the pro-
gress made during the past year. There were eleven
competitors for the prizes, and the one that got the
lowest number of marks would be a credit to any parish
in Ireland; nay, very few, if any parish, had such a
well-tilled garden, stocked with such a variety of fruits
and vegetables. There was one cause for regret, and
that was, he was unable to give a prize to each com-
petitor, for they all deserved one. However, all were
rewarded quite sufficiently by the beauty of their homes
and the abundance of dainty and nutritious food they
now have for themselves and their families. For many
months past they had rhubarb or stewed gooseberries at
one of their meals every day, and fruits, such as straw-
berries, currants, etc., and some now have apples ready
for use. What an improvement it is to them to have
peas, broad beans, parsnips, carrots, lettuce, etc., for
their meals in place of the white butterless bread and
the black liquid from stewed tea leaves of former times.’’

At a meeting at Warrenpoint on Sunday last, the
principal speaker, Mr. Jerry MacVeagh, M.P.; delivered
an indictment composed of facts and figures that cer-.
tainly justified him in saying that ‘‘any Ulster Unionist
should be ashamed to mention the word bigotry.” Two
Protestant Nationalist M.P.’s, Mr. Swift MacNpill and
Mr. Stephen Gwynn, were on the platform with Mr.
MacVeagh. Mr. MacVeagh said:—' With two such
men upon our platform to-day, how hollow sounds the:
squalid and infamous charge which the enemies of the
National movement are for ever whispering, that Irish

Catholics are the slaves of religious intolerance! There

. is no slander that does more deadly work in England,
and never was a baser or more lying charge uttered.”
However, the facts and figures which he gave in great
detail were the chief factors in his powerful indictment.
I it be true, as we are willing to believe, that no
slander does more deadly work in England, why not
meet the slander? Would it not be well if the U.I.
League published leaflets setting forth the state of
things in- Ireland for the information of Britishers?
There are a large percentage of Britishers, we feel sure,
who quite honestly believe the slanders levelled at the
Irish ““ Idolators” in the matter of bigotry.  Mr.
MacVeagh’s speech, revised for publication as a
pamphlet or leaflet, would be a wholesome thing to
distribute in England, and particularly amongst English
public men. We understand that the U.L.L. intend
doing something for the Irish industrial propaganda; a
series of leaflets controverting by facts and figures the
slanders that are honestly accepted in England concern-
ing Papists would be very useful—indeed, accord-
ing to Mr. MacVeagh, absolutely necessary. We
are so pre-occupied with affairs in Ireland that
we don’t bother about what they think or don’t
think in England. But we recognise that English
opinion, unfortunately, is a big factor in the shaping of
Irish affairs, and the slanderers who retail their stories
to the Big Brother ought to be met. A series of leaf-
lets might be well worth compiling. Mr. MacVeagh’s
speech would make an excellent leaflet by itself, and the
various  information that has appeared in the LEADER
from time to time—some of it collected in ‘‘ Three
Railways and a Bank ”’—is at the service of any body
or person who wishes to spread the truth.

Here are a few phrases from a ‘‘ Hungarian’’ organ
hurled at fellow-Irishmen—Mr. John Redmond’s ‘‘ mer-
cenary calumniators of honest men.” ¢ Every disso-
lute and foul-mouthed man subsisting on money sub-
scribed in America and Australia to aid the Home Rule
Cause.”” Ts it not pitiable to see the cause of Ireland
dragged in the dust by this sort of ' Hungarian’’
screeching at Irishmen? If men must differ, need they
go in for this sort of abusive language?

The poor ignorant Orangemen of the North are in a

sense to be pitied. One must remember the things that
these ignorant men are told by those who ought to know
better. At an Orange demonstration in Drumquin, in
Tyrone, one Major Hamilton, who must be an old man,
for he stated he was a member of the Orange Society
for fifty-nine years, said, according to a local paper:—
‘““Now they had a Government in power who would
give every office and every emolument to the Roman
Catholics, who were endeavouring to get the Protestants
ousted from every public position and appointment.’’
How can we blame the ignorant Orangemen of the rank
and file when that sort of stuff is harangued to them
from Orange platforms? -

According to tke same report, Brother the Rev. J. A.
Dayvis, of Dromore, said :—‘ Their loyalty to the Throne
of England was given conditionally that the Throne
was__always held by a Protestant.”” That’s fine
““loyalty * for you! - This rev. gentleman also said:—
““ Some people took ypon them at times to criticise their
action in regard to Their conditional loyalty, but they
did not mind this criticism, for they took for tLeir

THE LEADER.

Avucusr 31, 1907.

idance the teaching of God, and the people who
(g:ll'?cicised them knew nothing about the word of God, or

by whom they were being guided.”

. On the occasion of this meeting of poor ignorant
Orangemen an Orange Hall was formally opened. Sir
James H. Stronge, Bart., performed the ceremony. We
read in a local paper that he expressed the Lope that
it might be ‘the home of true religion and sound
politics.””  Though from a national point of view tke
narrow-mindedness of these unhappy or lnlggulded
Orangemen is tragic, we cannot refrain from smiling at
“the home of true religion and sound politics !

Weo see by an advertisement of the official Martynette
organisation called ““ The National Council,”” that Rule
VII. states:—No member of tke British armed forces
or pensioner therefrom shall be eligible for membership,
nor shall any person who has otherwise taken an oath of
allegiance to the British Government be eligible so long
as he shall retain the office or position involving that
oath.”” Well, Sir Thomas Esmonde, who, amid great
applause, was proposed for memberskip of this so-called
““National Council”” by Mr. Arthur Griffith, the
budding statistician, is a Justice of the Peace, and a
Justice of the Peace has to take an oath. Perhaps the
sccretary of the organisation would explain this little
matter.

We don’t see much wisdom in the resolution passed
by the North Dublin Union, and that is apparently
going tke rounds of the other Unions, that all the child-
ren christened in the workhouses be given Irish names,
and that the names be entered in the Irish form on the
register. This seems to us commencing very much at
the wrong end. If the Gaelic League tas so far unfor-
tunately failed to get as big a grip on the commercial
and professional classes, does anyone think—anyone who
knows Ireland as she is, with all her prejudices, social
castes, and the rest—that a rule making it obligatory
that every child born in a worklLouse must have an Irish
name, will tend to make Irish names popular outside
workhouses? This move seems to us most ill-advised
and short-sighted.  If it is carried out in the work-
houses—which we doubt—its effect, so long as the great
body of persons as at present are not giving Irish names
and registering them in Irish characters, will be to-
wards pauperising Irisk: names.

Is it worth compulsorily putting Irish names on
children born in workhouses' if the good sense and
patriotism of the more fortunate people do not impel
them to put Irish names on their children? If profes-
sional men stick to Algernon and Bertie, does
anyone think that it will tend to change matters by
putting Pdopars and "Donnead, €ivlin and Maipe on
the unfortunate children who, by no fault of their own,
come into the world subject to the jurisdictions of our
patriotic P.L.G.’s, who show their love for Ire-
land by calling their hopefuls Algernon and Beatrice,
and the poor workhouse infant Tavg ?

. If the Irish-Treland movement is not real enough to
mduce.peop'le of their own volition to put Irish names
on their children, it will not mend matters, but rather
make matters worse, to bind workhouse authorities to
put Irish names on all children who have the misfortunie
to be born in a workhouse,

. Mesers. Gill and Son have just published a school-
book by *Oonnéao O Loitigpig on Irish Composition.
The price is 1s. 3d. A prominent figure in the language
movement writes of it:—'“ Mr. Denis Lynch’s services
to the Irish language movement are by no means recent.
Th'ey go back to some of the earliest phases of the
existence of the Gaelic League. One need not, there-
fore, feel surprised at the appearance of his recent book,

Complete Course of Irish Composition for Intermediate
and other Schools.” This publication, the letterpress
of which is excellent aund very olear, will certainly
supply a great want.. The exercises are well ar-
ranged, and contain a good store of common idioms.
The section on Irish Prosody is probably unique in
books of the kind, and gives concise information about
both the older syllabic metres and the more recent
assonantal varieties of versification. I have no doubt
that Mr. Lynch’s book will be of great use to sll
teachers of Irish in our schools and colleges.’’

We have veceived Gacctpa Fiolla an  Amapdin

(Fracpa Eitsead). This is the text prescribed for the
June, 1908, Examinations in Junior Grade Honours and

Oatmeal is ‘a favourite morning dish with most
Americans. We have jjust heard of an Irish-American
who, when at home in America, was accustomed to
have Irish meal at his breakfast every morning. He
recently came across to Ireland on a visit, and naturally
expected it would be an easy matter to get Irish oatmeal
in an Irish hotel. On asking for McCann and Hill’s
oatmeal, however, at the hotel in which he was staying,
he was astonished to be informed that they had no
Irish meal, but that they had—oats—of foreign manu-
facture. We have no doubt that our American friend
saw many other things that astonished him in this
abnormal country duting his stay, but at any rate he
would have seen more of them six or seven years ago.
1f we are not marching, we are at least creeping on.

We have received the following :—

‘“ Parting is such sweet sorrow.”’—Dear Mr. Editor,—
The idea amongst our Sinn Fein friends appears to be
tkat the withdrawal of the Irish Nationalist members
from the House of Commons would be so sensational an
affair that it would alarm John Bull, scandalise him
before the nations, cause him to feel ashamed of him-
self, and dispose him to think that his wisest course
would he to make large concessions to the Irish claims.
Those gentlemen have a higher opinion of John tkan I
have. I regard him as a hardened old sinner who has
no sense of shame, and who could not possibly be brought
to blusk: for or repent of any of his ill deeds. Apparently
our friends think that in the parting scene between him
and. our National representatives he would manifest
much tender emotion and plead earnestly for a con-
tinuance of the old relations. Perhaps some one of them
on ‘returning home might picture his behaviour in the
following variant of a well-known Englisk song :—

““The tear fell gently from his eye,
The day we left him on the sktore;
His bosom heaved with many a sigh
To think he ne’er might see us more.
“Dear boys,” he cried, ‘ and can you haste away?
My heart will break ; a little longer stay!
T really cannot—cannot part with ye—.
Remain, remain! Don’t mind Sinn Fein!
“Abide with me!””

FHE LEADER. 19

For my pait I do not think the leave-taking would be
at all likely to proceed on those lines. I think the fol-
lowing stanza would come much nearer to a veracious
report :— .

‘“ A merry twinkle lit his eye,
The day we parted on the shore;
‘ Dear boys,” he said, ‘I cannot sigh or cry,
Although I ne’er may see you more.
1If go you will, there’s danger in delay,
Your minds might change; you’d befter haste away ;
Good speed be yours, alike on land and sea !
Don’t miss you train ; oh, sweet Sinn Fein !
How glad I’ll be!” ”’

Let me add that, except with regard to this one pro-
ject or plank of their policy, I am heartily witk: the Sinn
Feiners. They are patriotic and earnest men who—if
they will give up this impracticable idea—can render
in\r(r]nense assistance in the noble work of Irishising Ire-
and.

The ‘‘Tungarians’’ got a rather contemptuous re-
ception from the Dublin County Council. Mr. P. J.
O’Neill said :—“ A more puerile policy than this it was
hard to imagine.”” He also referred to the ‘“ Hun-
garian ”’ policy as ‘‘a will-o’-the-wisp which was being
trailed through the air by certain politicians at the
present moment.’”” Mr. Joseph Mooney, wko has not a
high opinion of the Party as it is managed, has, how-
ever, nothing but contempt for Mr. Martyn’s nostrum.
Mr. Mooney said:— A good many people thought it
(the Party) needed improvement, and the fact that it
was badly conducted had given to some gentlemen with
ncnsensical ideas, and who did not know what they
wanted, considerable stimulus.”’

A correspondent writes:—‘‘ A Chara—Apropos of the
celebration of the eighth birthday of the LEaper, T may
mention a little incident which occurred when it was
yet in its infancy. Some hundred and fifty students
were gatlered together in a class hall in Maynooth under
the present President of the College, who was our Pro-
fessor. A written question had been left in the ros-
trum,” and Dr. Mannix, in answering it, mentioned the
new paper, the LEspER. The name was well received,
and Dr. Mannix expressed his belief ¢ that any one who
continued to read it could not fail to be a good Irish-
man.” This was greeted witk great applause, for
though, owing to the extensive College studies, we were
not permitted to have or read papers,’ still most of
us managed to see a few copies of the LEapEr. Dr.
Mannix has probably forgotten this remark, but it im-
pressed itself so clearly on my mind that I have often
pondered on it since. I think time has proved how well
founded was Dr. Mannix’s belief. No doubt many
agencies have contributed something toward the creation
of the new life which has manifested itself in so many
forms in Ireland during these latter years, but I think
most people will admit that the LEsDER has done more
for the regeneration of our country than any other
journal. Not only has it done a giant’s part in the
creating of an Irish-Ireland, but now that the too ardent
spirits of some enthusiasts seem to carry them away a
little too fast and betray them into saying and doing
foolish things, the LEADER appears to be the only Gaelic
paper to hold sane opinions on the most important
questions of nationality as presented to us to-day. It
is the anchor of hope which promises to keep some of
our most earnest but too eager workers from rushing

We have received a copy of a new prayer-book 
:whic? is, we understand, the first publication of the 
kind bearing the imprint of the Irish Trade Mark. It 
was compiled, printed and bound in Ireland, and is 
published by the well-known Catholic publishing firm, 
Messrs. James Duffy and Co., Ltd. The manual has 
the imprimatur of His Grace the Archbishop of Dublin, 
and may be had in various bindings ranging from 1~. 6d: 
to 4s. 6d. in price. 
Some special features in it include. 
prayers for festivals throughout the year for the days 
on which they occur. 
List of Patron Saints of Irish 
dioceses and Roman Calendar for the year. It contains 
many hymns familiar and original, and several new 
prayers, litanies and devotions. 
The manual is en-
titled " Moments with Heaven," and under this title 
some beautiful lines appea1• in it composed by Brian 
O'Higgins, who has won fame in the Anglo-Irish lJoetry 
of Irish-Ireland. We quote the following typical verEe • 
from Mr. O'Higgins' expressive poem:-
" When sin from a soul is driven, 
When vice under foot is trod, 
·These be moments with Heaven-
Moments with Mary a~d God." 
The members of the cycling club attached to the 
Rathmines and Rathgar Catholic Association had an 
enjoyable trip to Kilmacud on Sunday week last. 
Ar_nongst the visitors staying at St. Kevin's House were

some Gaelic Leaguers from England, w4o enjoyed the 
Irish singing · and dancing items contributed by some 
members of the cycling club at an impromptu concert 
after tea. The social evenings of the Association will be

A well-known Dublin citizen called to our offices on 
Monday. 
He wished to s~ow us pa~·t. of a_private le~ter 
he had received from a fnend dom1c1led m the ·Umted 
States. 
The letter is dated August 14th. 
In the 
course of this private letter the writer says:-" Poor 
Ireland! The situation, from my point of view, is hope-
less. 
All that is best and most sturdy in the country-
the men who would be in charge if the country itself 
had control of itself-never raise a finger to help her. 
Politics there seem so parochial, so insular, that every-
thing written and spoken there on the subject is thread-
bare and of no depth. 
Unquestionably, the ablest man 
in Ireland at present is D. P. Moran, Editor of the 
LEADER; and the greatest service done for Ireland in 
recent years has been by his paper. You see the dif-
ference between Moran and others over there is that 
Moran knows what he wants, knows what he is going to 
say, and says it. 
The others, fo~· the most part, talk at 
random. 
Was ever anything so infantile as the sugges-
tion to submit Ireland's claim to the Hague? ' Now, tell 
me, was there 1 
Why, man alive, the ridicule -which 
that called forth, here and elsewhere, amongst persons 
of thought whose opinion shapes the common destiny, 
was very damaging to Ireland. 
Poor Ireland!"

had ne:ver before struck anyone that turning out "ladies" 
and snobs could never make for the uplifting of a 
country ·where men and women were so badly needed, or 
that, if anyone ever' gave the matter a thought, we 
-C?~ld go . on for ever feeding the professions and the 
c1v~l service and the bankers an~ the nurses and shop-

that the " tony " training gave for running away from

I think that the greatest. crime that posterity will lay 
at the door of the Irish Parliamentary Party is that 
long after the necessity for concentration of action had 
ceased, they systematically tried to kill e~ery other., 
movement tending to reform. We all know the land 
question had to be settled, and dual ownership got rid 
of before anything else bad a chance of a look-in in 
this country, and we also know that this question could 
never have been settled otherwise than by the land 
agitation and a fighting party; but unless parliamen-
tarians contend that, like Sequah or Beecham's pills, 
politics are an infallible specific for all the ills that Irish 
. life is heir to, totally different conditions apply now 
that the land que§tion is settled by Act of Parliament, 
and that minor details are all a matter of arrangement. 
The Party 1:-as been, and always can be, a most useful 
lever to remove obstructions to national development, 
and ·the sooner the country relegates it to its proper 
position as a lever and not an obstruction, the sooner

THE DEPARTMENT IN BEING.

VIII.—BALLYHAITSE, CO. CAVAN.

There. is a station on the G.N, Railway line called
Ballyhaise, and the village of Ballyhaise is about three
and a half miles away! Why the Great Northern Rail-
way Company call the station Ballyhaise is more than
we can tell, but should any benighted traveller with
a bag, an overcoat, and other traveller’s etceteras take
the G.N. Railway at its word and get out at Ballyhaise
station expecting to walk down to the village, he might
consult his lawyer as to whether or not he has cause
for an action for damages against this particular nation-
killing railway. Ballyhaise agricultural station is three
miles from the railway, the village is half a mile further
on. The drive from the railway fiction called Bally-.
haise to the Department’s station is over a partly

| Awgvst 31, 1907,

bogey, and partly wooded country, but it is relieved
by the driver’s recital of the wonderful performances
and virtues of the mare—we don’t think the driver
would ever tire relating them to you.

Ballyhaise is so out of the way that we had the
experience of spending a night 'in an agricultural
station.. We met a friend of ‘ours who is now a real
live M.P. at Amiens Street, and he had the pleasure of
travelling with us as far as Dundalk. Ho was on his
Wway to make a great speech somewhere, and he
threatened to ‘““ask a question in the House’’ if we
.gob a night’s lodgings at the expense of the Depart-
ment. When this article reaches his eye we expect he
will move the adjournment of the Fouse so as to discuss
the matter as it deserves!

The house attached to the agricultural station is a
mansion, a mansion of the type that would defy Crom-
well to lay low. It was built somewhere about the year
1760 by one Colonel Newburg. Somewhere about 1800
it passed into the hands of a Mr. Humphreys, a wealthy
Dublin cloth merchant, and in 1905 it went, as so many
other things before it went, “ under the Department.”’
However, it isn’t past history that concerns us at Bally-
haise. Whoever built it, it is a remarkable structure,
standing, like the Munster Institute, on one side of a
valley. You hear the eternal streaming of the Annalee
like a low wind, or sometimes like rain beating on the
slates; in the early morning you hear the rattle of the
milk carts on the way to the Ballyhaise creamery, a
big concern that we only had a glance at from the
outside. It is on the other side of the river, and has
no connection with the Department.

Some of the arched ceilings of Ballyhaise house are
indeed things of beauty. The house is a ready-made
that is also a good fit for its present purposes. Had
‘the place to be built to-day it would be difficult to do
it better, but, no doubt, the walls would have been
much thinner. We understand that the Department
purchased the place on very reasonable terms.

The Ballyhaise farm covers about 800 acres, some
150 of which are wooded. There are about 550 acres
-of arable land, 130 of which are under a four course
rotation ; viz., first year oats, second roots, thkird oats
or wheat, fourth hay. There are 100 acres of meadow,
and the remainder, about 300 acres, is kept under
Ppermanent pasture. In this way the land which is
‘most suitable for tillage is kept under tillage, while the
Teavy and hilly land which would be difficult and ex-
pensive to labour is kept under grass. Part of the
farm is marshy and waste, but the Department in-
‘tend to drain and reclaim it. The Principal at
-the station is Mr. Crockett, from the County Donegal ;
My. Prendergast, a College of Science man, is the literary
master, and Mr. Sherrard, a graduate of Glasnevin farm,
+ i~ the garden expert and lecturer; the farm manager is
Mr. Simpson, from the inevitable land o’ cakes. Mr.
‘Simpson takes charge of the boys when they are out
in the fields as well as looks after the management of
tthe farm. Mr. Corson is the cheese expert, and he has
two pupils specially learning this profitable industry.
It appears ungallant to place the professors before the
matron, bubt when we are concerned with an agricultural
station the- agriculturists naturally come first.  Miss
‘Stack Las five maid servants to look after as well as to
act as matron of this big establishment. She evidently
‘knows how to bind up a wound, for she was called away
during dinner to one of the men about, the place—there
-are ‘about nineteen labourers attached to Ballyhaise—
who got a nasty gash on one of his arms. The poor
fellow injured himself badly with a reaping hook.

On the evening, or rather night, of our arrival, for
‘it was about seven when we got there, Mr. Crockett
‘took us round the building. There is electric light
everywhere, even in.the cattle byres. There are two
fine lectures halls, a couple of dormitories, a spacious
‘refectory. The whole place looks spick and span, and

. -above all things massive. The yard and out-buildings

-at the back present the appearance of a barrack square.
“The main front entrance leads into a great hall which
is used as a billiard-room, where the apprentices and
the staff can amuse themselves during their leisure
‘hours.  Let us add, by way of anticipating any cry
‘aboub extravagance, thaf-the billiard-table went with
-the mansion when the Department took it over. It
-must have heen getting on for ten o’clock when we got

THE LEADER, S

back to the billiard-room after our look through the
house and some of the out-buildings, and two of the
staff were playing away. Proficiency at billiards, it
has been said, is an infallible sign of a mis-spent youth,
so, needless to say, we are not adepts at the game.
We were beaten in the two games that we played, but
it can honestly be said that the display ‘made”by our op-
vponenty at the table in no semse could be taken as
casting any reflection on the manner in which their
youth was spent!

On Friday morning after breakfast we went imme-
diately to have a look at the cheese-making. The
cheese-instructor was away, but the two young men who
are specialising in cheese were working hard. - They
were operating on about ninety gallons of milk in the
checse vat. One gallon of milk gives about one Ib. of
curd and, of course, the whey, so that it is really a
more profitable way of working milk than butter-
making. Tt does not require very much special plant,
only a vat and a few presses. The creameries that
already haye an engine could easily take it up. A few
store rooms were packed with cheeses waiting the time
when they will be ripe for marketing in Dublin and
Belfast. Cheese-making requires a good deal of atten-
tion, but if an outsider might express an opinion from
what he observed, there is no reason why this industry
should not spread rapidly over the country. Ireland is
not much of a cheese-eating country, yet in 1905 we
imported cheese to the value of £106,847. But, of
course, cheese producers need not depend on the home
market, and the United Kingdom imported cheese to
the value of £6,339,811 from foreign countries, par-
ticularly Canada, U.S.A., and Holland, during the
year 1905.

From the cheese-makers we walked up the farm to
where the pigs are. The piggeries at Ballyhaise are
on a big scale, and the pigs are all under experiment
of one kind or another. The Yorkshire pig
and the native pig and a cross between the
two are there in litters. Their food is weighed and
priced, so that the cost of the pigs is known up to the
time when a Committee in Belfast report on the animals.
We were detained for quite a long time in the piggeries,
for the rain had commenced to come down in torrents,
and it rained and rained the entire day, and a walk
round and about the 800 acres was out of the question.
The garden over which Mr. Sherrard presides didn’t
look its best in the drizzling rain and slop. They have
not yet adopted the plan of giving over a plot to the
care of each student, but that, no doubt, will he done
in due course. S

There are seventy cows on the farm, and we forget.
how many calves. The byre accommodation is to be
extended, and the stock is to be raised to a hundred
Lead of milk cows. About a dozen of the bull calves
will be kept each year, and fattened off at eighteen
months. About twenty-five of the best heifer calves will
be kept each year. These will be producing milk at
three years old, and will be brought in to fill the place
of old and unprofitable cows. The place is therefore
being run on systematic dairy-farming lines, i.e., each
year twenty-five heifers are brought in, so that no cow
will be kept for more than four years. The milking
qualities of tlLe stock will thus be improved in a shoxb
time, as a record of each cow’s milk is kept, and the
heifer calves from the best milking cows will always be
chosen. © The boys, or rather the young men,
have to take their turn at the various practical work
about the farm as well as attending the class work,
where they learn about seeds, manures, warble flies,
and the rest. We did not see many of the students,
they were scattered about. On Friday they, or some
of them, were told off to make ensilage, but whether
or not the dreadful weather drove them back in good
order to their comfortable woodwork department w
cannot say. Personally, on such a day we would pre-
fer to be making a potota-sprouting box indoors than
making ensilage out under the rain. Later in the

session came from various parts of Ireland, from the

Ba}lyhaise·is an interesting place, and we enjoyed our 
visit there very much. ' It was a pity that F,riday was 
such a wet day, for otherwise we could have seen more 
of the farm. 
As we drove back in the evening to the 
railway, the driver was apologetic about the weather. 
"Ah," said he, "the worst of Cavan is it'-s always 
raining."

OF PIGS-AN IRISH CONTROVERSY. 
Dear Sir,-I wish to bring to the notice of your able 
and influential journal, which I read with renewed 
pleasure each week, ·an abuse no doubt of small propor-
tions, but widely extended and easy of remedy. It is 
my experience that the old cruel custom of forcing a 
solid ring into a pig's snout still persists in many parts 
of the country. 
I need not describe the agonised 
screechi~g which accompanies the cruel operation. This 
causes unnecessary pain to our dumb friends. By the 
use of the modern form of • patent ring this cruelty 
could be avoided or largely obviated. 
When we see 
pictures of Paddy and his pig, I am sure no Irishman 
would like to feel that· the latter was suffering. 
Much 
of our national prosperity depends on the bacon trade. 
Thanking you in advance for inserting this,-I remain 
yours sincerely, 
PHILosus.

O to Ardmore on the Sunday nearest the 24th

way home. And so you come prepared to be more glad

nearly dead out. You expect to find an odd. old man
or woman at the holy well doing the rounds, with per-
haps, in addition, a sprinkling of young people about
the village on pleasure bent—no more. You will have
your eyes opened for you.

Ardmore is not what you would be likely to make
the mistake of taking for a rich place. Nor a modern.
That steam yacht, swinging with the turning tide, at
anchor in the bay, nor that big motor with its internal
rumble as of indigestion, perspiring on its gouty wheels

belong to natives of the village. That square, white
mansion flashing in the sun on yonder headland would,
if it moved nearer, lend an air of some prosperity to
the settlement, but as it is, it seems coldly watchful to
keep itself and its skirting grounds in genteel and dis-
tant dignity from danger of any contact with its poor
neighbours. Finally, the bleak, spreading strand with
no one bathing—and with no bathing accommodation
but salt water and loneliness—the sob of the sea, the
high-road with big gaps eaten out of it by the tide, the
low, simple houses clustering at the foot of the hill, and,

HE goodly paper-tax is many years gone, and has
left us in an age of gluttony in type. The less
of meaning sheltered in the type, the more fiercely
ravenous the gourmandising. Due to the leaning down-
wards that is in us all, and to an earth-wide fashion of
teaching laziness of brain through a method of educa:
tion whose aim is above all things to keep the child
from tkinking. Dictionaries are pillaged (in that
system) for Anglo-Saxon mono- and dissyllables, so that
tiny manlings and womanlings may be enabled to
swallow indigestible chunks of information with the
smallest exertion possible; garish pictures are
shouldered in and flung, with .the adult’s customary
ignorance of childhood’s wants, at the unformed and
healthy juvenile mind, which, consequently, catches the
significance of the plrases set before it with a lightning
semi-look. From the beginning, therefore, the civi-
lised youngster is taught, absolutely taught, a habit of
cursory reading; imbued, as early as can be, with a
sensitive repugnance to any out-of-the-way intellectual
effort. Tlence arises, as has been said, the majority’s
oceanic craying for what is comically dubbed ‘bad’’
literature, and which, in reality, is no literature at all.
This insatiable longing for watery scribbling has, neces-
sarily, its influence on the writers, and so we have tke
witless, greedy pant to get into print, whether anything
wcrth saying is to be said or not when print is reached.

The feather-heads, then, who cannot face live litera-
ture are not to be altogether blamed if a piece of tough
thinking simply addles them. Iis education and the
maodern popular autkor, who is far too gentlemanly to
write anything but suggestive slang, have led him to
read where no mental work is called for. Accordingly,
in his sluggishness, he often mistakes shallowness for
lucidity and lucidity for shallowness, obscurity for depth
and depth for obscurity. — Thus the eloquent empty-
neddle mints, while the honourable artist pawns his
classics. The mercurial skimmer of books does as muck:
harm as the books he dotes on. He is morally bound,
bound at least in honour, to curb his levity. There is
a kind of reading, however, where the critical faculties
must be keen-eyed; where the mtellect is frequently
asked to tussle with a wiry phrase; where the judg-
ment must now and then pause to give its yea or
nay to a new and lusty assertion; where tke ear is
forced to linger on the sweet-sounding fall and rise of
a noble period; where the sinuous sentence must be
even re-read if all its rich sense is to be drawn out of
it; and which is profitable and pleasurable and virtuous.
Bearing on the latter class of reading, a few truths,
known to many, but vastly forgotten, may be indicated
to those who are self-knowing and unprejudiced.

Here it may be mentioned that in what follows by the .
word ‘“ Literature >’ is meant the literature represented
by artists such as—to use names that are time-laurelled
—Dante, or Schiller, or Calderon, or Beaumarchais.

Literature is Art; and Art is the due expression of
the harmony of the universe. Harmony is proportion,
order. The harmony of the universe consists in the
exquisitely arranged way in which all beings, Creator
and created, grow, are or act. There are not different
kinds of harmony. Harmony, in this light, is absolute ;
the same in all things. It is the demonstration of that
Karmony, or, phrasing it differently, harmony displayed
in giving form to some object that delights our eyes
or ears, and forms a pleasant sight or pleasant sound.
A neatly-finished yacht, serenely taking the waters like
a swan, thrills us because of the proportion wkich its

AN IRISH-AMERI6AN ON TITLES AND
DEGREES.

Having a few weeks in this delightful county which
abounds with springs and is famous for its scenery, you
meet with people from every country and discuss every

. imaginable topic.

My attention one day was called to absurdities in
the old land from quarters which think themselves
perfect, that is, the system of knighthood and the prac-
tice of conferring University degrees.

We read that in olden times, those who are conver-
sant with Sir Walter Scott’s ‘Ivanhoe’ and
‘“ Kenilworth,”” or who have followed ‘‘Arthur’s
Knights ’ through Sir Thos. Mallory’s quaint pages,
who have kept the vigil fasting with Amadis de Gaul,
in all seriousness, and with Don Quixote, will never be
reconciled to the modern practice of knighting worthy
plump, non-belligerents of easy habits, such as the
thirty gentlemen who were dubbed knights by their
peaceful Monarch, Edward VII., in honour of his birth-
day. 5

]y%nighthcod, as anyone conversant with romance in
old times knows was a distinctly military honour. The
aspirant served first as a page about the person of a
great lady, and there acquired graces which were deemed
becoming a knight. : .

Then he went to the wars, and after performing
various valorous feats, was deemed worthy of being
tapped by his Sovereign with the flat of his sword and
bidden rise, Sir Nicholas Blount, Sir Walter Raleigh,
or Sir Thos. Downes, as the case may be.

How Don Quixote would mourn tiese decadent times

- were he to read the list of knighthoods conferred re-
cently. ot 4 5
Foremost among the new knights are Mr. R. Cremer,
M.P., and Professor John Rays, of Oxford, the former
for services in the cause of universal peace, and the
. latter, who attended as Oxford’s representative at the
~ Universal Peace Conference in New York—the thought
. should make the warlike knights of medizval times turn
* over in their graves. Amnother is the learned principal
of a Scotch University, still another is the head of a
steamship company; two others painters, more fit to
grace a court than camp. Another still is the ingenious
“W. S. Gilbert, a jester and minstrel, a composer of
‘amusing satirical masques and plays, who would a
ousand years ago be most popular in some great lord’s
; he it was who wrote the ‘‘ Pinafore,”” and who
ted jestingly ‘with the Lords of the Admiralty.
also wrote the words of the ‘ Mikado,”” and to show
ity of the powers that conferred honour on him of

THE LEADER. %

knighthood, “they would not let the opera be performed
in England while the Mikado’s relative was visiting
there, but immediately on his departure gave him the
title of Sir. i

The list is filled up with Mayors, Aldermen, and
prosperous merchants—not, a soldier amongst them.
Look at some of our Dublin knights, whose only dis-
tinction is to vote against the wishes of the people, and
who excelled in brewing, distilling; also others in the
selling of the same liquids to impoverish and debauch
their countrymen. We also have some peaceful bakers,
who would have a more appropriate title as master of
the rolls. T even met at Queenstown an estimable
feamale who tended bar and would dole out a peacefull
glass of . porter, and she was the wife of a knight.
They have done away also the solemn vigil in the Castle
Chapel, for there is no castle or chapel where the fat,
gouty merchants and aldermen could endure a whole
night on bended knees without their good eating and
drinking and their usual night’s sleep. These things
make the ordinary American citizen laugh at the tom-
foolery of monarchy and the conferring of the univer-
sity degrees, in which the ordinary mortal expects a little
consistency from authorities through their learning, is
even more ridiculous; for iustance, how often do you
see soldiers, many who could scarcely read or write,
getting the honorary degrees of D.C.L. or LL.D., simply
because they killed off a few savages who fought bravely
for their rights. If old Churchill, the first Duke of
Marlborough, whose illiteracy was remarkable, was
alive, the old women of Trinity would go on their.
knees and make him an LL.D. Though possibly the
great Father Burke, whose name is a household word
in all America, as well as the English speaking people
of the world, and whose oratory in the dead languages
for two weeks daily at the great Council in Rome before
all the famous Churchmen of the world, won for him
from Pius IX. the title of Prince of Preachers. Was
he offered an LL.D. by Trinity, which considered them-
selves liberal; no, he was not. And even this same
bigoted fossil institution in old times, as any reader of
the life of Edmund Burke can see, educated him, and,
because he tried to do justice to his country, refused
him the merited degree until late in his life. Then his
name and fame were so illustrious that they relented for
the sake of an advertisement, and even now a person
sees a statue to him inside rails of institution.

If Treland ever gets a Catholic University, the hope
will be that degrees honorary will be conferred on de-
serving, and people worthy of title. They ought also
impress on their graduates that a degree is not a suffi-
cient mark of success, as Edmund Burke got along all
right without it till end of time, and almost all the
prominent men of this great Republic, both business
men and soldiers, as well as great merchants, never had
a College degree. .

You will excuse me for trespassing on your valuable
space, but that England which clings so tenaciously to
old customs and old institutions, should have accepted,
as it has, this bastard tradition of making knights of
actors, playwrights, and College professors, is no less
appropriate than making LL.D. and D.C.L.’s of suc-
cessful soldiers.

In the case of the language revival, there was opposi-

aries, pamphlets, books, have sprung up like mushrooms,

forthcoming from the Boards of Education, and it is not
too much to expect that the next gemeration of Trish-
men will be bilingual.

How has this been accomplished? By organisation.
An organisation that soon got in touch with every
parish in Ireland was formed by the Gaelic League.
Ttinerant lecturers, propagandists, and teachers were
sent broadcast throughout the land. Representative
men kept hammering away at Parliament, at the
Treasury, at the Boards of Education, at the managers,
and, as a result, their requests are steadily, if slowly,
being granted.

What has Lappened in the case of the industrial move-

amount of natural popularity; it had for its motto,
¢ Trishmen help one another.” All that was required

foreign manufacture—and only when the Irish article
was as good and as cheap. But, strange to relate,
Trishmen are, as a nation, not doing so—not a tenth

sation, it will fail—at least for years—to reach the
bulk of the people. In a word, the LeApER must geb

: My suggestion is that a campaign should be started

BLACKROCK COLLEGE, C.S.5p., BLACKROCK.

BOARDERS return on 2nd September. DAY SCHOLARS
must be present at 10 a.m. on 3rd, when classes are resumed.

By its long and brilliant record of success, Blackrock has shown
that it affords it students the highest educational advantages;
while its beautiful grounds, buildings and position, just overlook-
ing Dublin Bay, give it a combination of attractions which are
quite unique.  Last year, in the Intermediate Exams., it obtained
18 Exhibitions, 3 Senior, 7 Middle, 8 Junior, 33 distinctions, 131

passes.
14 SCHOLARSHIPS (£350) to be competed for on 30th August.

THE UNIVERSITY & CIVIL SERVICE COLLEGE,

A distinct institution, and the only residential College of its kind
in Ireland, affords young men an opportunity of receiving a higher
University and Civil Service training under proper religious safe-
guards and away from the dangerous seductions of city life. In

addition to its well-known University successes, Blackrock has

obtained this year:—Indian Civil Service, Jas. Sweeney; Indian

Police, Jn. Slattery; Nat. School Inspectorship, J. Doody; Army

Accounts, M1. Flanagan; Exchequer Dept., T. Casey; Sandhurst,
